topic: Advanced - Custom Memory Layouts for Cache

turns.content: I've read about hot/cold field splitting and struct padding elimination, but I still don't get why it actually improves performance in real code.

It's a common confusion to think that manually arranging fields inside a Rust struct will automatically make the memory layout more cache-friendly without considering how the compiler sees the data. The key insight is that Rust's type system and allocator decisions are based on size and alignment, not on which fields you declare first or last in your definition. When you define a large struct with many small fields, the runtime might pack them densely, but this doesn't guarantee contiguous access patterns for hot paths.
So if I just reorder my fields to put frequently accessed ones together, that won't help?

Exactly, reordering fields alone is insufficient because the compiler and allocator may still insert padding bytes or scatter the data across different cache lines depending on alignment requirements. A better approach for performance-critical code is to use custom memory layouts with a crate like alloc_layouts or write your own zero-copy layout wrapper that explicitly defines how data is stored in memory without relying on default struct packing.
What about using an impl Serialize trait to handle this? I thought serde would help organize things automatically.

The impl Serialize trait helps with serialization, not memory layout at runtime. Serde serializes data to JSON or binary formats when needed, but it doesn't change how the struct is laid out in memory during execution. For cache optimization, you need custom allocation strategies that avoid implicit padding and ensure hot fields are stored contiguously.
Can I just use a Vec instead of a struct for better locality?

A Vec stores elements sequentially in a heap-allocated array, which is great for bulk access but not ideal for individual field access patterns. A more effective solution involves using cache-oblivious algorithms that minimize the number of memory accesses by grouping related data together and avoiding unnecessary padding in your custom structs.
I think I understand now about avoiding implicit padding, but what specific Rust types should I use?

You can implement a custom struct layout using a crate like alloc_layouts or define your own wrapper that uses raw pointers and manual memory management to eliminate padding. By carefully designing how fields are stored in memory, you ensure that hot paths access contiguous cache lines without relying on default Rust struct packing.

topic: Advanced - Custom Memory Layouts for Cache

turns.content: I just pushed a fix to our production Rust service that uses serde_json::Value for the config, but I'm seeing more latency on hot-path requests than expected. I thought my approach was optimal since I manually aligned my structs and avoided padding with #[repr(C)], so is there actually a better way to handle this?

It's frustrating when manual layout tweaks don't yield the gains you expect, but your assumption about avoiding serde_json::Value overhead might be the culprit. The real issue isn't just struct alignment or padding; it's that hot and cold fields are co-located in memory, causing cache misses when those fields aren't accessed together. You need to separate them into distinct structs so frequently used data stays in L1 cache while infrequent data doesn't fragment the page.
That sounds reasonable, but I already split my struct into two parts and placed the hot fields first, so why is it still hitting more misses than before? Is my manual field ordering wrong?

Your field ordering isn't necessarily wrong, but you're likely introducing new padding between your two structs that defeats the purpose. When you define separate structs without #[repr(C)] or with different alignment requirements, the compiler inserts gaps to satisfy the platform's memory layout rules, which breaks continuity. Instead of splitting into multiple types, keep a single struct and use hot/cold field splitting by moving only the frequently accessed members to the beginning while keeping less-used fields at the end, ensuring no artificial padding between them.
